title: L'Oreille qui Gratte
subtitle: Brand identity for a punk, DIY, anti-establishment webradio.

blocks.title: Overview
blocks.body: L'Oreille qui Gratte is a punk, DIY, anti-establishment webradio. Fanzine aesthetics, scratchy typography, colors that aggress. Nothing smooth — and that's the point. This was my first real graphic charter: a brand identity built entirely around the refusal of brand identity.

blocks.text: Punk isn't the absence of a system. It's a system that refuses everyone else's.

blocks.title: The Challenge
blocks.body: How do you bring order to chaos without killing the chaos? Building strict rules for a brand whose identity rests on rejecting rules felt like a contradiction. That tension turned out to be the brief itself.

blocks.title: Process
blocks.body: I spent weeks constructing a graphic charter that codified the uncoded — defining which typefaces scratch, which color combinations aggress, how to build layouts that feel deliberately broken without actually falling apart. The fanzine reference was the key insight: fanzines aren't random. They're obsessive in their DIY logic. That rigor became the framework.

blocks.text: A strong identity isn't beautiful. It's immediately recognizable.

blocks.title: Outcome
blocks.body: First graphic charter. First real visual identity project. A lot of mistakes — even more learnings. What stayed with me: consistency is what transforms a style into a brand. The rules don't kill the punk. They protect it.
